data.title: Heartland Value Fund Q4 2025 Attribution Analysis & Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Heartland Value Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Manager Commentary.

In the fourth quarter, the Heartland Value Fund gained 2.61%, compared with the 3.26% return for the Russell 2000® Value Index (RUJ). While the Fund slightly underperformed its benchmark in the quarter, it has outpaced the index over the past one, three, five, and 10 years and since inception in 1984.

During the quarter, stock selection accounted for most of our underperformance relative to the benchmark. Health Care accounted for nearly all the negative selection effect. Our results were more the outcome of what we didn’t own than our actual holdings in the sector. Biotech companies in the benchmark soared in October and November, which is a good example of the speculation which persists. However, we don’t have direct exposure to this group, as many are profitless. We are, though, actively examining our Health Care stocks as several were among our portfolio’s biggest detractors in the quarter.

An example of a Health Care holding that struggled in the quarter was Integer Holdings Corporation (ITGR), which specializes in the design, development, and production of medical technologies and components. In late October, Integer’s stock sank more than 30% after the company significantly lowered its outlook for growth and margin expansion in 2026, as several products experienced slower market adoption than was expected. But management expects these headwinds to be short-lived, with a robust portfolio of higher growth and margin products coming to market, which should allow the company to get back on track. The company still expects to outgrow its industry peers by 2% while growing earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) at twice the rate of sales. Even better, the company announced a $200 million stock repurchase authorization after the selloff, increasing our conviction that the shares are materially undervalued. This confidence drove us to increase our exposure to Integer in the quarter.

Another position we increased was Columbus McKinnon (CMCO), which designs, manufactures, and distributes materials, handling products, and systems used in a variety of industrial applications, including lifting, automation, and precision conveyance. Early this year, CMCO reported slower-than-expected Q3 results, citing slowing industry demand driven by U.S. policy uncertainty. By itself, that would have likely triggered a mid-single-digit to high-single-digit selloff based on historic price reactions. However, management also announced a $2.7 billion acquisition of a competitor, Kito Crosby, which sent the stock plummeting more than 40% due to concerns surrounding execution and an increased leverage profile upon closing.

At current prices, we believe Columbus has a compelling risk/reward profile as management seeks to reduce leverage and improve the growth and margin profile of the consolidated business. Management is targeting mid-20% EBITDA margins upon integration, up from 15.5% in FY25 pre-acquisition. In our opinion, CMCO can rerate closer to its industrial peers that trade at an average of 12x EV/EBITDA. Meanwhile, our view of the stock’s attractive valuations seems to be validated by aggressive insider buying by management in the aftermath of the Q3 selloff.

A good illustration that true value can be realized with patience is Alexander & Baldwin (ALEX). When we originally initiated a position in this Hawaii-based real estate investment trust in the summer of 2024, we were significantly underweight in our Real Estate exposure, relative to our benchmark. In addition, the sector was the third-largest contributor to our portfolio’s “active risk” at the time. So, we sought to expand our exposure to the sector but were looking to do so in a cautious way.

From a risk/reward standpoint, ALEX looked favorable to most other REITs at the time. We liked how ALEX divested a non-core construction business, making this a pure-play REIT with assets strictly in Hawaii. And we were drawn to historically low industrial vacancies in the State as well as the company’s strong absorption rate relative to its peers.

Over the past six months, however, Alexander & Baldwin shares sold off materially over concerns of slowing tourism in Hawaii. This is despite the fact that little of ALEX’s properties are on resorts or directly exposed to travel. Actual real estate fundamentals in the Aloha State remain strong with industrial vacancies hovering at just around 2%. The company also enjoyed a robust pipeline of pre-leased developments to drive earnings growth and a strong balance sheet with below-average leverage, at 3.5x net debt to EBITDA.

Nevertheless, the stock was down more than 14% through the first two months of the quarter — until Dec. 8, when a group of investors agreed to take the company private at a 40% premium, which was close to our evaluation of fair value. While ALEX wasn’t a huge winner, the takeout boosted the holding’s performance to close out the year and demonstrated that other investors are starting to pay attention to the companies we focus on.

©2025 Heartland Advisors | 790 N. Water Street, Suite 1200, Milwaukee, WI 53202 | Business Office: 414-347-7777 | Financial Professionals: 888-505-5180 | Individual Investors: 800-432-7856

data.title: Nexus Industrial REIT: The Future Is Cautiously Bright
data.description:
Nexus Industrial REIT (NXR.UN:CA) is a Canadian pure-play industrial REIT. It recently disposed of its commercial and retail assets to focus solely on improving its existing industrial properties. As a result of this change in strategy, I believe management has taken some of the risk off the table, and with time, its stock price will gain momentum to move higher.

Nexus Industrial REIT: Past Year Updates

In the past year, there have been recent developments positioning Nexus Industrial REIT to be on the right track:

  • In August 2025, it sells 16 retail/office properties, resulting in $51M of proceeds
  • Some of the proceeds from the disposition are being used to develop its 325,000 SF expansion at St. Thomas, Ontario, and a 115,000 SF industrial building in Calgary, Alberta
  • In December 2025, Nexus Industrial REIT resumes its industrial acquisition program by acquiring 2 industrial properties in Quebec for $40.1 million. The anticipated stabilized market rate will be 10.4%. The transaction was funded from its credit facility.
  • In the past year, a 115,000 SF small bay industrial building at 102 Avenue Southwest, Calgary, was built on speculation. Its speculative bet paid off, and now 8/9 units are leased up, of which 5 are now firm. Once stabilized, the property is anticipated to produce an 11% unlevered return.
  • 150,000 SF industrial warehouse in Hamilton. The building currently sits vacant and is expected to generate a 5.9% yield. Nexus owns 80% of the building.
  • In Aug 2025, through its financial statement disclosures, Nexus Industrial REIT had increased credit facilities by a total of $360M:
    • Unsecured credit facilities increased by $160M from $625M to $785M
    • Term loan facility from $175M to $200M
    • Total revolving facility from $440M to $575M

The F-Score Continues to Be Just Okay

In reviewing its financial statements, the company's F-Score is listed below. The F-Score measures the financial strength of the company's balance sheet and income statements:

A score of 9 indicates a financially strong company, while a score closer to 0 suggests the company is financially weak. Nexus Industrial REIT hovers in the middle with a score of around 5–6.

One of the reasons for its low score is the accounting treatment of its Class B LP units and the fair market value of its industrial buildings. On an annual or periodic basis, it revalues these units/buildings, and if there is a fair market value loss, it has to reflect this in the income statements. This lowers the net income and creates a "fake" or notional loss. Although the company is operating as usual and collecting rents from its tenants, the fair market value adjustments are distorting its profitability. This is evident when net income in Q1-2025 had declined drastically to Q2-2025 (from $33,151 to -7,625 the following quarter), while its AFFO figures from quarter to quarter have been consistent, ranging from $14,000 to $16,000 each quarter.

Another hit to its F-Score is also Nexus Industrial REIT's quarterly issuance of new shares. Whenever the outstanding share count is more than the prior quarter/year, the F-Score takes a point off. However, issuing new shares is how management acquires new properties. It issues new shares to the property owners it is acquiring the property from. So this also distorts the F-Score downwards.

Another risk to also consider is it's not fully clear yet what the negative impact is on the loss of NOI from selling its portfolio of commercial/retail properties. There is a chance the payout ratio will have increased slightly in its Q4-2025 financials.

The Future is Cautiously Bright

Despite these risks, several factors may be working in the REIT's favor:

  • Nexus Industrial REIT is resuming its industrial acquisition program and purchasing assets that are accretive to its bottom line
  • The REIT is also internally upgrading its properties to increase NOI and locking into longer lease terms with existing tenants
  • It has increased its borrowing capacity by 25%, hinting at further acquisitions and developments in the near future
  • The current Canadian industrial market is taking a "wait and see" approach
    • This could mean some investors are sitting on the sidelines, giving more opportunities for Nexus Industrial REIT to acquire properties.
    • Overall vacancy rates across Canada remain flat, implying industrial tenants are showing resiliency to US tariffs and current economic conditions.
  • Nexus Industrial REIT had reduced its risk exposure by selling off its commercial/retail assets. Previously, management may have stretched itself too thin by being overly focused on more than one asset class. By refocusing its efforts on industrial assets alone, it no longer needs to deal with retail/commercial tenant turnovers, which are typically higher than in industrial tenants.

AFFO is a Concern, but a Mild One

Since 2023, Nexus Industrial REIT's AFFO payout has been more than 100%. This means cash has been going out faster than it is coming in:

A fellow Seeking Alpha writer had also highlighted this risk a few months ago. What is driving the higher than 100% AFFO payouts, I believe, are its Class B LP units. Whenever Nexus Industrial REIT acquires new properties, it issues Class B LP units to the property seller. It is set up this way to keep the debt on Nexus Industrial REIT's balance sheet low, and because these units are kept one level above the Nexus Industrial REIT's financial books, it also does not impact NOI.

To be clear, it is not structured this way to manipulate the books but to incentivize property owners to sell to Nexus Industrial REIT and to allow sellers to participate in the monthly REIT distributions.

In looking at Q3-2025, its payout ratio was 107%; this slippage amounts to a little more than $2M that quarter. For now, Nexus Industrial REIT can manage this payout, and I believe over time, as it engages in more accretive acquisitions and through tenant step rents, the payout ratio should steadily decline.

Investors Should Be Patient Here

It was only August 2025 when Nexus Industrial REIT had shed its portfolio of commercial/retail properties. Investors should give management time as it realigns its strategy to be a pure-play industrial player.

Its long-term debt has been below 50% for the past few years, which shows the balance sheet has bandwidth to make further acquisitions. Since the REIT is a growth-oriented operator, management will most likely never increase its dividends even if its payout ratio returns to sub-100%.

However, industrial assets are typically a much safer asset class than retail and commercial. Also, management seems to have a knack for finding properties that are accretive and boost its bottom line. If investors can buy into this investment thesis, then the current stock price could be considered undervalued. If investors are willing to show patience and give management time to roll out its industrial pure-play strategy, then in the long run, the stock will start increasing again.

I'm bullish on Nexus Industrial REIT.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge Growth Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Evan Bauman, Aram Green & Amanda Leithe, CFA

Balance Matters When Markets Rotate - Market Overview

U.S. equities delivered resilient but volatile performance in the fourth quarter. While major indexes remained close to all-time highs, market conditions shifted meaningfully beneath the surface. Momentum-driven themes tied to artificial intelligence (AI), technology and cryptocurrencies extended into the early part of the quarter before reversing, resulting in heightened volatility and greater stock-level dispersion.

The S&P 500 Index returned 2.7% during the quarter, while the benchmark Russell Midcap Growth Index declined 3.7%. As volatility increased, investors became more selective, rewarding companies with durable fundamentals, innovation-driven growth and strong execution, while penalizing stocks where expectations had moved ahead of near-term fundamentals.

Against this backdrop, the ClearBridge Growth Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, marking its third consecutive quarter of outperformance. Results reflected participation in early-quarter momentum — particularly from AI-related exposures — combined with strong downside protection as risk appetite moderated later in the period. We believe this outcome underscores the benefits of a balanced approach to growth investing.

2025 Year in Review

Looking back, 2025 was characterized by a wide range of market conditions that tested portfolio construction and reinforced the importance of discipline and flexibility. The year began with elevated uncertainty and the broad market endured a sharp correction from mid-February through early April, followed by a powerful rebound driven by enthusiasm around AI and innovation-led growth. After hitting an interim high in late October, markets became more volatile with rapid rotations and reversals putting a premium on portfolio diversity and our ability as managers to remain nimble, tactical and convicted in our decision-making.

Overall, the Strategy delivered a strong year, outperforming its benchmark by nearly 600 basis points (gross of fees). Market outcomes among the mid and large cap stocks we target increasingly reflected idiosyncratic drivers rather than broad factor exposure, rewarding companies investing in innovation, improving productivity and executing well against long-term growth opportunities.

In a market where much of the upside is priced in, stock selection matters more than ever.

Importantly, 2025 also demonstrated the benefits of a multiyear evolution of the portfolio. While our core philosophy remains unchanged after more than four decades, the Strategy today reflects a more balanced expression of growth. The steady compounders that continue to make up the base of our portfolio can provide effective downside capture for investors, while some of the disruptive growth names that have been added over the past few years have helped us better keep pace in a rising market. The portfolio is also more diversified across sectors and subsectors to better balance conviction with risk management. We believe this positioning has improved the portfolio’s resilience while preserving its ability to capture long-term upside for clients.

Quarterly Performance

Fourth-quarter outperformance was driven by a combination of balanced portfolio construction and strong stock selection. Early in the quarter, momentum tied to AI and technology supported results, while the portfolio’s exposure to steadier compounders and defensive growth names helped protect performance as volatility increased.

Within IT, Shopify (SHOP), an e-commerce platform that enables merchants to operate and scale digital storefronts, benefited from continued adoption by larger enterprises and improving monetization across its ecosystem. Broadcom, a leading semiconductor company and long-term holding, continued to execute well amid strong demand for custom silicon supporting AI workloads. TE Connectivity (TEL), an electronic component manufacturer specializing in connectivity and sensor solutions, also contributed, supported by secular trends in its automotive and industrial businesses.

While communication services was the greatest detractor within the benchmark, our differentiated positioning supported relative outperformance. For example, a number of portfolio holdings in sports and live media, such as Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS), Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE) and TKO Group (TKO), were positive contributors in the quarter.

However, select holdings in consumer staples and health care did face pressure. Following a period of strong performance, shares of e.l.f. Beauty (ELF), a cosmetics company focused on affordable, digitally native products, declined as sales growth was hurt by management’s decision to stop shipments to retailers that were slow to pass through tariff-related price increases. That said, we are encouraged that consumption trends for the brand remain healthy and management is already seeing shipment growth recover in the current quarter. In health care, Doximity, a digital platform serving medical professionals, traded lower as higher-beta growth stocks within health care experienced renewed volatility.

Positive contributions from steadier growth holdings helped to offset these pressures. In the consumer discretionary sector, TJX Companies (TJX), an off-price retailer, delivered solid third-quarter results and had a strong start to the fourth quarter as better merchandise availability yielded broader and higher-quality assortments that supported traffic, same-store sales and earnings growth. Our consumer performance also benefited from the recent addition of On Holding (ONON), a premium athletic footwear and apparel company gaining share globally. On Holding delivered an impressive quarter punctuated by strong growth in international markets, such as China, as well as accelerating growth from apparel. Likewise, within health care, top individual contributor Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) benefited from growing optimism around its kidney disease pipeline and continued to demonstrate the value of idiosyncratic, innovation-driven growth.

Portfolio Positioning

Overall, 2025 was a period of strong idea generation that saw the Strategy reallocate capital toward opportunities with more attractive risk-reward profiles. These included new positions in merchant power producer Vistra (VST), biotech company Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) and global hospitality company Hilton (HLT), among others. Staying true to our sell discipline, we also exited a number of holdings where we had diminished conviction in the company’s growth outlook such as health care provider UnitedHealth Group and audiovisual technology company Dolby.

Additionally, we looked for opportunities to elevate the quality of the portfolio. Within industrials, for instance, we rotated our exposure in the fourth quarter from Old Dominion Freight Line, a leading less-than-truckload (LTL) carrier, into XPO (XPO), the fourth-largest LTL provider in North America. We remain mindful of the still-challenging macro environment for freight and see greater idiosyncratic growth potential at XPO. Under new leadership, the company is improving service levels, pricing discipline and margins, leaving more room for outperformance should macro malaise persist.

We also continued to use our profitable larger positions in Broadcom (AVGO) and Robinhood Markets (HOOD) to opportunistically fund more attractively valued holdings. Most recently this included adds to Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), a global copper producer and one of our top contributors for the quarter, following a price drawdown related to an operational incident late in the third quarter. We believe management handled the situation effectively, and the dislocation reinforced our view that duration can be an advantage when owning high-quality assets tied to long-term secular demand.

Outlook

Our outlook remains consistent with what we articulated earlier in the year — and importantly, what played out in the fourth quarter. Markets remain resilient, but volatility has increased as momentum-driven areas cooled and stock-level dispersion widened, reinforcing the importance of disciplined stock selection and a balanced approach to portfolio construction.

AI continues to represent a powerful long-term opportunity, though early beneficiaries such as semiconductors and infrastructure have already seen significant gains. We are focused on making sure we have not only the right exposure within the AI complex but also other offensive bets in the portfolio to position the Strategy well should market leadership broaden further. At the same time, we continue to emphasize balance, owning companies not only with offensive growth potential but also defensive characteristics built on strong free cash flow, clean balance sheets and proven management teams, which should help to protect the portfolio should the environment weaken.

We remain opportunistic — taking advantage of pockets when stocks are oversold and trimming when enthusiasm runs ahead of fundamentals — while staying committed to a bottom-up, high active share, and long-term-oriented approach. We believe this discipline positions the Strategy well to navigate continued volatility, identify durable winners beyond broad market trends, and drive long-term performance for clients.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Growth Strategy outperformed its Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter. On a relative basis, the Strategy had positive contributions from two of the nine sectors in which it was invested. The contributors were the consumer discretionary and materials sectors, while the consumer staples and information technology (IT) sectors detracted the most.

On a relative basis, overall stock contributed to performance, while sector allocation effects detracted. Stock selection in the IT, communication services and consumer discretionary sectors, as well as an overweight to the materials sector, benefited performance. Conversely, stock selection in the consumer staples, financials and health care sectors, overweights to the communication services and IT sectors and an underweight to the consumer discretionary sector weighed on performance.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to relative returns were Vertex Pharmaceuticals, Broadcom, Freeport-McMoRan, TE Connectivity and an underweight to Roblox (RBLX). The largest detractors from relative returns were e.l.f. Beauty, Doximity, Robinhood Markets and a lack of exposure to Rocket Lab (RKLB) and Expedia (EXPE).

Evan Bauman, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Aram Green, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Amanda Leithe, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Copyright © 2025 ClearBridge Investments. All opinions and data included in this commentary are as of the publication date and are subject to change. The opinions and views expressed herein are of the author and may differ from other portfolio managers or the firm as a whole, and are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. This information should not be used as the sole basis to make any investment decision. The statistics have been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the accuracy and completeness of this information cannot be guaranteed. Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Source: London Stock Exchange Group plc and its group undertakings (collectively, the “LSE Group”). © LSE Group 2026. Original Post

data.title: The Unstoppable Freight Giant: Why Wabtec Is A Must-Own In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies Corporation (WAB), in my opinion, has one of the best business models in the market today. Even though individuals typically don’t directly interact with it, it serves a very important role in the economy. Being the largest manufacturer of diesel-electric freight locomotives in North America, it makes sure that goods are transported efficiently across the U.S., Canada, and Mexico. Before the acquisition of General Electric’s (GE) Transportation division in 2019, the firm was known for primarily supplying brake systems for trains. After the deal was completed, Wabtec now provides equipment and services for locomotives throughout their whole lifecycle, which can be quite long. In this article, I will go over my thesis for the firm, sharing with you my conviction and reasoning.

Thesis

The North American industry for producing freight locomotives is very concentrated. Wabtec operates as one side of a duopoly it has with Progress Rail (CAT), while currently leading its competitor in both new deliveries as well as the existing installed base of locomotives and rail car components. That directly increases future revenues for services like repairs and overhauls, which usually come with very good margins.

The second segment that Wabtec operates in is the public transit industry. Here, the company supplies systems and components for all major networks around the world. I expect urbanization trends to continue going forward, which will help the firm as the demand for its transit products stays high.

The last point I’m making is that Wabtec is currently in a leading position to manufacture fully electric locomotives for freight purposes, with large orders already secured for its FLXdrive model.

Dominating The Rail Freight Industry

Looking at the past, General Electric and EMD have been the two dominant manufacturers of diesel-electric locomotives that Class-I railways are using to transport heavy goods. EMD is part of Progress Rail, which belongs to Caterpillar (CAT). GE locomotives were originally produced by the large General Electric conglomerate. However, after GE broke up the company into several separate firms, it also sold its locomotive division to Wabtec in 2019.

The deal that cost Wabtec approximately $11bn made the firm the leading player in the industry. An antitrust lawsuit against Wabtec, filed by its main competitor Progress Rail in 2023, showed that Wabtec's market share sits at roughly 71% of all new locomotives, with an even higher 90% share of all U.S. Tier IV-compliant locomotives that have lower emissions. They also have a global installed base of over 24,000 locomotives. All Class I railroads in North America are using Wabtec models as the core of their fleets.

Having a large installed base is a huge benefit for the business. Selling new locomotives is more cyclical, because my railroads are growing and reducing their fleets based on demand. This can impact earnings in a material way, since macroeconomic factors are impacting these demand behaviors. When orders are coming in however, they are often very large. In September of 2025, the national railway of Kazakhstan ordered locomotives worth $4.2bn, which represents the largest order in Wabtec's history. This also shows that international regions are likely going to grow faster in terms of orders compared with the North American market, which creates upside for the business.

The most important argument in my investment case, however, is related to the large installed base. Wabtec can benefit from the long productive life of a locomotive by continuously servicing it, which can include different types of offerings.

Since locomotives are running for hours, regular maintenance is important to ensure safety. These include inspections of braking systems, the engines, or important electronics. Customers of Wabtec are often signing Long-Term-Service agreements over several years to keep their fleets operational. It is important to say that many components and parts inside of a locomotive are proprietary, which means that they can only be supplied by the original manufacturer. This creates high switching costs and secures Wabtec revenues over the entire lifespan of the locomotive.

A different type of aftermarket service is the modernization of older locomotives. This is the most profitable type of service. Instead of buying a new locomotive, the customer upgrades the ones it already has. For example, switching from DC systems to AC power or implementing a more efficient battery system can extend the life of an older model significantly, while costing the railway just a fraction.

Wabtec also offers customers digital upgrades for their older models. For example, predictive maintenance sensors can improve downtimes, while optimization software like Trip Optimizer can help save fuel, which incentivizes owners of older fleets to upgrade their locomotives to save costs. Because some of these software features come with a subscription model, Wabtec is receiving recurring revenues that are highly predictable.

Speaking of recurring revenues, as of their Q3 2025 earnings report, out of the total $5.913bn of sales in the Freight segment, roughly 71% belong to services, component deliveries and digital intelligence offerings, while just 29% are from equipment deliveries. Out of these 71%, I would assume the majority is highly recurring, as I explained above, while just a fraction should come from one time sales.

Because margins of service offerings are typically much higher compared with the original sale of the locomotive, primarily due to higher pricing power and lower manufacturing costs, Wabtec is actively focusing on this part of the business. With the acquisition of Evident's Inspection Technologies Division in January of 2025, the digital side of the business should continue to grow fast (over 40% y/y as of Q3 2025!).

Wabtec doesn't just manufacture locomotives. Their core expertise before acquiring GE's Transportation business was in manufacturing braking systems, which explains their long-form name. This is still an important business to the company, as it supplies manufacturers of rail cars with necessary components. These braking systems naturally wear out over time, which again gives Wabtec the chance to service these systems at high margins.

Public Transit As A Growth Driver

Wabtec's second segment is Transit. Within it, the company supplies systems and components for public transit vehicles like subways or regional trains. However, it does not manufacture the trains itself. Even though expansion projects for transit systems are dependent on public budgets, this segment can generally be seen as less cyclical, providing diversification for the overall company. At approximately 28% of total revenues, it is much smaller compared with Freight, but still is important to the investment thesis.

On top of its leading braking systems, Wabtec produces doors air conditioning systems for trains as well as overhead power line pantographs. Because public transport trains have short start-stop cycles, brakes and doors do wear out faster, making maintenance highly important. Servicing is currently sitting at around 55% of total Transit revenues.

Besides general trends like urbanization and decarbonization, which are increasing sales in the public transportation market, the same digital solutions that are helping the Freight segment grow profitably are positively impacting the Transit segment as well.

Just a week ago, Wabtec secured a $386m order from New York's MTA to deliver hybrid battery-diesel work locomotives. This shows Wabtec's importance in also providing advanced locomotives outside its core freight market.

The Future Is Green

With emissions becoming a bigger factor in designing a locomotive, both Wabtec and Progress Rail are developing the next generation of highly efficient, battery-electric models. Wabtec leads that race with the first-ever heavy-haul locomotive powered entirely by batteries. Its newest generation FLXdrive has a battery capacity of 7 MWh, which is as much as approximately 100 Tesla (TSLA) Model S batteries combined. However, these new locomotives can't fully operate on their own. Instead, they work in a so called Hybrid-Consist. In this scenario, one FLXdrive locomotive works between two or three traditional diesel locomotives. The FLXdrive uses regenerative braking systems that store energy when braking or driving down a hill, and redeploy it when starting or going uphill.

In theory, FLXdrive locomotives could move a train on their own, but their battery capacity wouldn't be sufficient for a full freight route. This is why they are often used in specific roles like working as Shunters or Switchers in rail yards, or working in combination with traditional models. In a Hybrid-Consist scenario, they can reduce fuel consumption as much as 40% compared with a fully diesel powered train, while also lowering emissions materially. The goal is to reduce the share of needed diesel locomotives in that hybrid setup to a point, where electric locomotives can fully operate on their own.

Valuation

To determine whether Wabtec is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) EBITDA Margin (TTM)
Wabtec (WAB) 17.79 21.56%
Caterpillar (CAT) 24.88 21.58%
Knorr-Bremse (KNRRY) 12.99 12.43%
Alstom (ALSMY) 8.13 6.47%
The Greenbrier Companies (GBX) 8.41 13.05%
Average 14.44 15.02%

Using an EV/EBITDA relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of rail infrastructure companies, which is 14.44 at the time of this article.

As we can see, Wabtec trades at a slight premium to its peers. However, its profitability metrics are far superior compared with competitors. In fact, Wabtec only has one direct competitor in the Freight segment, which is EMD. Even though selling locomotives isn't exactly Caterpillar's focus today, I believe that the two firms are quite similar in their business model. Both are selling large, expensive machines that require constant maintenance and upgrades long after the initial purchase. While both the railway business as well as the construction and mining industries are experiencing phases of cyclicality, customers in these industries are valuing availability of their machines a lot, which leads to aftermarket pricing power, giving them similar profitability numbers.

In general, I believe that the high degree of predictability, resulting from a large backlog of over $25bn, together with a leading market position in a highly concentrated industry, is justifying the slight premium based on the quality of the business.

Risks to Consider

The primary risk of an investment in Wabtec is related to its dependence on macroeconomic developments. Weaker consumption demand is directly impacting rail freight volumes, which leads to potentially lower purchases of new locomotives as well as a less frequent need for maintenance services.

Another important risk are technology changes. If fully electric locomotives become necessary to comply with emission regulations, Wabtec needs to deliver a capable locomotive, or otherwise its competitor EMD might overtake them.

While the freight locomotive industry is highly concentrated, the transit sector is generally more competitive. Since this part of the business is an important growth driver going forward, Wabtec needs to keep or expand its market share to keep the thesis intact.

One last risk is related to autonomous trucking. Currently, moving freight by rail is much more cost-efficient over long distances than trucking. However, if autonomous electric trucking were to make significant progress, it could potentially create an alternative to the rail.

Conclusion

At a time when the market is primarily focused on technology stocks, traditional industrial companies like Wabtec seem uninspiring. However, the firm in my opinion has one of the best business models in the market. With its combination of locomotive sales in a highly concentrated market and high-margin service revenues from aftermarket sales, the firm has perfected the razor-and-blades model that makes it such a great holding for the long-term. At a reasonable valuation considering its quality, I would actively buy at these levels. As a consequence, I initiate coverage on Wabtec with a Buy rating

data.title: Intapp: A Great Value Play Amid ARR Acceleration
data.description:
As we head deeper into the new year and prepare for the Q4 earnings season, the market is looking incredibly tricky. Investors are showing wariness at high valuations, particularly in the tech sector, while macroeconomic conditions and geopolitical headlines also make the current landscape unpredictable.

My solution to investing in choppy markets remains to prioritize investing in small- and mid-cap, more value-oriented stocks. These "growth at a reasonable price" names lagged their large-cap peers over the past year, and I'm betting on a rotation of market leadership to drive the group up. Intapp (INTA), meanwhile, looks quite compelling after a recent correction. The software company, which specializes in solutions for the financial services industry, has lost a whopping 45% of its market value over the past year, even as fundamentals have remained solid.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Intapp in August, when the stock was hovering around $43 per share. I upgraded Intapp from a previous neutral stance, owing to the company's recent improvement in growth trends, coupled with margin expansion as well as an enlargement of the company's TAM. Amid the fresh leg downward, I'm taking advantage of the downside to bolster my position, and I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Despite the recent downside in the stock, Intapp has actually given the markets a "beat and raise" in its most recent quarter. Since value is such a large part of the appeal in this stock, let's start with a discussion of the company's latest valuation multiples against its latest expectations.

At current share prices near $38, Intapp trades at a market cap of $3.14 billion. Netting off the $273.4 million of cash on Intapp's latest balance sheet (free of debt) gives us an enterprise value of $2.87 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Intapp has slightly boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $569.3-$573.3 million in revenue, reflecting a 13%-14% growth range that is slightly improved from $566.7-$570.7 million (12%-13%) previously. It's also now expecting a midpoint 17.5% pro forma operating margin, up 30bps from a previous 17.2% expectation and putting the company at a respectable "Rule of 30" threshold (though not quite a "Rule of 40" stock as many software firms aspire to).

Meanwhile, for FY26, Wall Street is expecting Intapp to deliver $648.1 million in revenue, or 13% y/y growth. This positions Intapp's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.0x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue

We can find very few companies that are growing at a mid-teens pace that trade at <5x forward revenue multiples. Given the company's rising margin profile, its attractive sector niche (financial services), and, in particular, rising SaaS ARR and remaining performance obligations, we think this is an opportune time to enter into this stock.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Intapp's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q1 (September quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Intapp's revenue grew 17% y/y to $139.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $135.3 million (+14% y/y) by a three-point margin. We note that growth largely kept pace versus 18% growth in Q4.

There are several indicators to support Intapp's improving growth rates. First, note that Intapp is facing headwinds from its SaaS transition (albeit, a little later than most other cloud companies are facing). As a reminder, cloud transitions are typically a headwind to revenue since license deals generate more revenue upfront, while SaaS subscriptions are recognized ratably over time. Intapp is shifting more of its customers away from licenses and into the cloud, which will spread out this revenue over a longer arc of time.

The underlying cloud ARR (annual recurring revenue), meanwhile, is growing 30% y/y to $401.4 million, while total ARR (inclusive of recurring elements of the legacy license deals) still grew at 21% y/y to $504.1 million, both outpacing revenue growth. In other words, Intapp is planting the seeds for a larger revenue base down the line.

Also: net revenue retention rates are improving, up to 121% in Q1 (slightly better than 120% in Q4). Many software companies have struggled with churn and customer downgrades in a tighter IT budget environment; Intapp's improving NRR is a signal that the company is succeeding at cross-selling and up-selling motions.

We also note that remaining performance obligations, essentially a measure of backlog for a recurring software company, grew 30% y/y to $715.2 million. As a reminder, Wall Street is currently expecting Intapp to hit $648 million in revenue next year. Intapp already has more than this amount of revenue in backlog. There are a number of growth catalysts driving this shift, including Intapp's expansion of products plus its growing penetration into a new vertical: commercial real estate.

The company also notes its recent sales partnership with Microsoft Azure is bearing fruit. Per CEO John Hall's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We continue to grow our high-impact partner ecosystem, anchored by Microsoft and a strategic set of 145 curated data technology and services partners. It's one of the most powerful vertical ecosystems in our industry. And its real differentiator is how deeply our partners are integrated into our commercial operations. They're strategic amplifiers of our business, enabling us to pursue larger opportunities, execute faster and scale more efficiently without a proportional increase in internal costs.

Again, Intapp doesn't fall within the "Rule of 40," but its combination of consistent revenue growth plus reliable margin expansion does give the stock a great growth/profitability balance.

Q2 look-ahead and key takeaways

The next major catalyst for Intapp will be its Q2 earnings release, expected in early or mid-February. To me, the key watch items are:

  • Revenue growth should continue to hold pace or even improve. Given ~30% growth in cloud ARR and RPO, Intapp shouldn't see any material deceleration in revenue growth, despite Wall Street's expectations of deceleration ahead. As license deals become a smaller portion of the business, the company's tailwinds from cloud conversion will help the y/y revenue comps and paint a more favorable picture for growth.
  • Repurchase activity. As noted previously, Intapp has quite a healthy balance sheet with nearly $0.3 billion of cash, without debt. Its rising margins are also leading to healthy cash flow. The company has been slowly buying back stock (~$50 million in the last quarter); we expect the company to step up repurchases as the share price falls.

All in all, Intapp remains a fantastic "growth at a reasonable price" investment to "Buy." Stay long here.

data.title: Global Stocks Set To Rally Again In 2026, Though U.S. Market May Regain Lead
data.description:
International stocks are set to extend a market rally from 2025 but may not outperform US equities again in 2026. The S&P Global Ex-US Broad Market Index (BMI), which tracks over 12,000 non-US stocks, gained more than 28% in 2025, outpacing returns of nearly 16% from both the S&P 500 and the S&P United States BMI, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

The S&P Developed Ex-US BMI, which tracks stocks in developed countries outside of the US, and the S&P Emerging BMI, which tracks stocks in emerging markets, also recorded gains of more than 31% and more than 20%, respectively, in 2025.

Several factors contributed to the outperformance of international stocks over US stocks in 2025, including a weaker US dollar. Investors also increasingly diversified capital in international markets amid global trade tensions and expansionary fiscal policies in certain countries. Though strong returns are again expected for international stocks, the US market may regain its lead in 2026.

"I would not anticipate an outperformance to the US markets," Frank Windels, head of equity capital markets syndicate at Truist Securities, told Market Intelligence. "However, the trend of higher returns compared to average returns should continue for the European and Asian markets. In particular, the new issue market and sectors in growth technology, including defense technology, continue to be focus areas for investors."

Investors will also be attracted to global equities as they seek to diversify beyond the AI-driven dominance and elevated valuations in the US market, Windels said. Western European markets, for example, could present opportunities from technology and defense stocks, the healthcare sector and IPOs, Windels said.

"With that said, the US market remains the deepest and most liquid," Windels said. "Any volatility in the broader market over the year will drive higher performance for the US market compared to the international markets. A year with steady corporate earnings growth and limited geopolitical surprises or tariff reprises should drive both US and non-US markets evenly with a tight performance band between the two categories."

Potential for emerging markets to outperform US

Emerging markets are positioned to record strong gains again in 2026, although "matching the pace of 2025 will be challenging, given the strong returns over the last 12 months," according to Gary Pzegeo, managing director and chief investment officer for CIBC Private Wealth.

"Supporting factors include resilient earnings growth, attractive valuations relative to developed markets and macroeconomic tailwinds such as a softening dollar and a likelihood of more US Federal Reserve interest rate easing," Pzegeo told Market Intelligence. "Challenges include geopolitical risk, potential volatility from trade and policy uncertainty and vulnerability to shifts in global growth or commodity prices."

The S&P United States BMI's aggregate daily forward price-to-earnings ratio ended 2025 at 23.15x, higher than a 15.33x multiple for the S&P Emerging BMI, according to Market Intelligence data. The ratio divides a stock's current price by estimates of future earnings per share, giving an insight into its perceived potential and relative cost.

In addition to robust earnings growth, discounted equity pricing and a softer dollar, there are further tailwinds that may push emerging market equities to ultimately outperform US stocks in 2026, Arun Bharath, partner and chief investment officer for Bel Air Investment Advisors, told Market Intelligence. These factors include higher income per capita growth and more prudent fiscal policy in emerging markets relative to the US.

Emerging market equities may also have an opportunity to again outpace US equities in 2026 if foreign investors drive capital outflows from the US amid the Trump administration's "America first" policies or if performance cools among AI-related stocks in the US, according to Bharath.

In particular, countries where stocks lagged global performance in 2025, such as India, could see stronger market performance in 2026.

"As a result of its underperformance, valuations on a forward multiple for Indian equities are more reasonable," Bharath said. "In the meantime, the Indian administration has enacted consumer-friendly policies that will likely boost economic growth in 2026."

Brazil's stock market may also exhibit robust performance as the country's central bank has the ability to lower interest rates from elevated levels to boost economic growth, Bharath said.

The Central Bank of Brazil's policy interest rate was 15% at the end of 2025.

Weaker dollar, rate easing to benefit emerging markets

Emerging market equities would particularly get support from continued weakness in the dollar. "A weaker dollar generally benefits emerging markets by lowering debt service costs for dollar-denominated liabilities and enhancing local currency returns for foreign investors," CIBC Private Wealth's Pzegeo said.

The US Dollar Index began 2025 above 108 but trended mostly between 96 and 100 during the second half of the year, according to Market Intelligence data.

Declining interest rates in the US will likely limit the dollar's upside in 2026. The US Federal Reserve lowered its benchmark rate six times from September 2024 through the end of 2025 amid cooling inflation and stable employment trends. Expectations for further rate cuts are mixed this year as the Fed weighs risks from sticky inflation and weakening labor market signals.

Markets project the Fed's current rate will decline by 25-75 basis points this year, according to CME Fedwatch data. "A US Fed tilting toward lower rates allows emerging market central banks to maintain expansionary policies," Pzegeo said.

US trade policy will add further pressure to the dollar in 2026, following a year in which emerging markets remained resilient despite new US tariffs. "Hawkish trade policy in the US is pushing central banks to diversify their reserve exposure away from dollars," Pzegeo said. "This technical headwind drove the initial move lower in the dollar."

However, bouts of dollar strength may emerge periodically throughout the year. "Episodes of dollar strength driven by risk aversion or unexpected macro surprises could temporarily pressure risk assets in emerging markets," Pzegeo said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Royal Bank of Canada: Displaying Its All-Weather Credentials
data.description:
A booming economy isn't a prerequisite for healthy bank earnings. Just look at Royal Bank of Canada (RY) (RY:CA) ('RBC') for proof. The Canadian economy hasn't been at its most robust recently, but that hasn't prevented the Bay Street titan from delivering impressive profits, with its adjusted return on equity approaching the 17% mark last year.

Of course, RBC has a knack for being an "all weather" kind of firm, as its dividend record attests to. The bank has paid a distribution in every year since 1870, and it hasn't cut it this side of the 2000s; the dot-com bubble, global financial crisis, COVID, and all the other economic bumps in that time evidently haven't caused it much bother.

My only real gripe with RBC is its valuation. Its latest display of resilience has seen it return around 40% since my opening piece last summer—a move that has lifted some of the stock's valuation metrics well beyond their historical norms. As much as there is to admire about its business, the price you pay does matter in investing, and on that basis, I will be keeping RBC on the watch list for now.

Hedges Come in Handy

(All financial figures are in Canadian dollars unless stated otherwise.)

While RBC has its fingers in many pies, Canadian personal and commercial banking is the core of its business. Not only did its Canadian Banking segment contribute nearly 50% ($9.74 billion) to firm-wide net income in RBC's fiscal 2025, but its return on equity was also well above the consolidated number, clocking in at nearly 22%. The bank achieved this despite the unhelpful backdrop of higher unemployment and sluggish GDP growth, which has continued to drive weaker credit metrics in this part of the business.

Residential mortgages account for the lion's share of Canadian Banking's loans. While there isn't much loss content there, assets like credit cards are a different beast, and loss rates have continued to climb in this book. As a result, credit charges within Canadian Banking rose around 30% last year to $3.65 billion.

While RBC has had to contend with higher provisioning costs, it has also generated more firepower to absorb that. Specifically, net interest income within the Canadian Banking segment rose 18% last year. Admittedly, a portion of that rise was inorganic, as the bank bought HSBC's (HSBC) Canadian business midway through its fiscal 2024. This made for an easier comp in the early part of 2025.

That said, a wider net interest margin has also played a role. I did mention one of the drivers of this last time, which is the bank's deposit hedging program. To quickly recap, RBC has a large stock of rate-insensitive deposits. These include the everyday accounts that Canadian households use to pay their bills, buy their groceries, and so on. Of course, using these deposits to fund variable-rate assets opens the bank up to a degree of interest rate risk. Hedging helps mitigate this.

Imagine the bank using these deposits to fund a laddered bond portfolio that's spread over, let's say, five years. As one year's tranche matures, another is bought to replace it. That way, the bank's interest income is less sensitive to immediate changes in interest rates. Because rates were very low a few years ago, this process has been beneficial recently - and management expects it to remain so in 2026.

In addition, lower interest rates are helping on the funding side of the ledger, as they've made products like Term accounts a bit less attractive. That's been good for RBC's domestic deposit mix - as a share of total deposits, non-interest-bearing balances increased 80 basis points last year based on period-end figures - and management also expects this tailwind to carry over into 2026.

Weighing all of that up, management thinks Canadian Banking can deliver 100-200 basis points of positive operating leverage this year. Assuming a cleaner year in terms of credit quality evolution, that should tee this business up nicely for 2026.

Diversification Does Its Job

Being a true universal bank has also worked nicely for RBC recently. For instance, while volatility may not be great for some parts of its business, it has been a boon for trading revenue, as shown below. Of course, there's a flip side to that coin, which is that a (relative) period of calm will probably see trading income fall back somewhat. If I had to be negative about one aspect of RBC's outlook, this would be it, albeit that will be an industry-wide issue.

RBC's Wealth Management unit has also been posting some good numbers of late. Remember, RBC isn't just a bank; it's also a leading investment manager. It had $1.56 trillion in assets under management in its Wealth Management segment at the end of 2025, up 17% year-on-year. It services over three times that number, with assets under administration exceeding $5.4 trillion. Rampant global stock markets have been great for these businesses, boosting AUM/A and, in turn, asset-based fees. On the back of this, RBC's Wealth segment posted $4.29 billion in net income last year, 25% more than in 2024.

Much like trading income, it's difficult to say how financial markets will perform in the near term. However, as you can see below, valuations do look stretched in crucial markets like the United States, which may become an issue at some point.

A Nice Problem

Last year, RBC earned a strong 2.84% return on its risk-weighted assets, climbing to over 2.9% in the fourth quarter. This degree of profitability is giving management a very nice problem: the bank's dividend distributions aren't enough to mop up its earnings, meaning the bank is steadily accumulating excess capital.

RBC ended last year with a 13.5% Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio, up 30 basis points year-on-year and 75 basis points above management's target range (12.5%-13.0%). Uncertainty being the theme of the times, management is opting to err on the side of caution, hence the surplus. Still, capital will only continue to build from here, and that will either necessitate greater cash returns to shareholders, M&A, or more organic growth. That being the case, the bank's recent dividend raise makes sense, with management boosting the quarterly payout by 6% (11% year-on-year) to $1.64 per share.

Richly Valued

The market's reward for this impressive display of resilience has, maybe deservedly, been a bout of valuation uplift, with these shares gaining around 35% since my last update to $234.96. That maps to 2.58 times RBC's book value, which is roughly 35% above the 10-year average.

Other metrics show something similar. For example, RBC made $14.43 per share in adjusted net income last year, for a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of over 16. This is also around 35% above its 10-year average. Similarly, its 2.6% trailing dividend yield is 33% below its 10-year norm.

Readers who practice dollar-cost averaging and/or dividend reinvestment might not care one bit about this; investing in periods of overvaluation is part and parcel of that strategy. However, for those looking to make a purchase outside of that, waiting for a pullback may be the prudent move from here. On that basis, I rate the shares a "Hold."

data.title: Gatekeeper Systems: FY 2025 Was Ugly, But The Backlog Tells A Different Story
data.description:
Investment thesis

Gatekeeper Systems (GSI:CA) (GKPRF) stock has experienced a 40% drawdown from its October 2025 high, partly because it traded at 10 times sales at that point and also because the full year 2025 ended with a 16% decrease in sales and a net loss, explained by the timing of new orders coming in after the fiscal year had already closed. Since the close of the fiscal year, the company has announced $43.8 million in new contracts compared to reported sales of $31.8 million, representing a multiple of 2.4 times 2026 sales if all contracts are reported this fiscal year and presenting a buying opportunity given this timing misunderstanding.

Business model

Gatekeeper Systems operates a security and surveillance platform focused on public transportation and school buses, using hardware such as security cameras and sensors to collect real time data. This data is used in proprietary software and also forms the basis for the company's high-margin hosted services. So rather than selling a single product, the company wants to create an ecosystem of hardware, software, and services designed to monitor and protect passenger vehicles, transitioning from one-off sales to recurring revenue.

As expected, the hardware doesn't have particularly high margins, but it serves as the entry point to the ecosystem. On top of this hardware layer are the software solutions that apply AI-assisted video analytics to security and even compliance issues, since something as minor as missing video from a security camera on a school bus or public transportation vehicle can lead to lawsuits against the operating institution. An even more attractive part of the business is the hosted services that Gatekeeper also offers, providing many features that customers might require in addition to the hardware, such as video storage, monitoring, analytics, automated enforcement, ticketing workflows, mobile Wi-Fi and even fleet management. These services are delivered from the company's own data centers, so management is targeting gross margins of around 90% over time (currently around 70%). Therefore, the value proposition isn't surveillance itself, but rather the data that transportation agencies, municipalities, and law enforcement can use to reduce security risks.

Historically, Gatekeeper specialized in school buses, a good market with stable growth and strict safety requirements, where it has already reached over 3,500 clients, representing approximately 25% of the US market share. However, this is a very niche market where it was difficult to maintain rapid growth, so since 2018 they have expanded into public transportation as well, a move that broadens the company's market. Currently, Gatekeeper serves over 60 public transportation clients, and the contracts won in February 2025 with the Toronto Transit Commission and in September 2025 with the Long Island Rail Road validate the product and begin building a reputation with government agencies, which is a crucial step for the company's future.

Valuation

At first glance, fiscal year 2025 looks disappointing, and maybe at second and third glance too because revenue decreased to $31.8 million, or -16% from $37.8 million in fiscal year 2024. A net loss of $3 million was also reported, so there's really little to like about the report. However, the fiscal year ended on August 31, 2025, and since then Gatekeeper has announced $43.8 million in new contracts, including the $27 million contract with New York MTA's Long Island Rail Road, a $9.3 million school bus contract, and several other smaller contracts. To put this figure into perspective, these newly announced contracts represent approximately 140% of fiscal year 2025 revenue, and while not all of this order backlog will translate into revenue immediately, if only half of it is reported as sales in 2026, we would already be talking about a 60% sales growth ($31.8 million reported in FY2025 + $22 million). Add the other $22 million in 2027, and sales would more than double in two years, not counting new contracts or recurring revenue.

So, assuming that after these two years of high growth the company maintains 20% annual growth and its net margin expands to 12% as software and services initiatives gain more weight in sales, the company would be earning around $16 million in net income in 2030. With a cash P/E ratio of 20x due to the high growth and stability of the business, the enterprise value would be around $316 million, but I think it's valuable to add the $14 million of net cash on hand; therefore, the market cap would ultimately reach $330 million. Divided by 95 million shares outstanding, which doesn't typically dilute or buy back, would result in a target share price of $3.50 Canadian dollars per share if purchased on the TSX Venture Exchange, or a compound annual return of 14%.

Risks

As observed in fiscal year 2025, Gatekeeper's revenue continues to rely heavily on hardware projects, and while the company is moving towards recurring revenue and subscriptions, these only represent around 10% of total sales at the close of fiscal year 2025. Therefore, I believe we can still expect lumpy sales and even some contract delays that will reduce the predictability of the business.

I also believe that, while Gatekeeper has expanded its customer base, a significant portion of its revenue still comes from a relatively small number of large transportation agencies and school transport providers, which will lead to customer concentration and a somewhat reduced addressable market that will be exposed to political decision-making and regulatory changes, institutions that are not easy to deal with.

The bottom line

The company has experienced a significant drawdown from the $3 Canadian dollar per share it reached in October 2025. I believe part of this decline is due to it being overvalued at that point because it was trading at 10 times sales, which is too optimistic for a company that is still 90% hardware. But another part may be due to the poor results reported at the close of fiscal year 2025, which were affected more by timing than by a deterioration in the business. Therefore, while I can't call it a bargain, at this price I might consider buying.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Small Caps Could Lead The Equity Market On Improving Credit Conditions
data.description:
Bond markets often move ahead of equities, particularly when it comes to shifts in economic momentum. Recently, corporate credit spreads have narrowed while government bond yields have eased, a combination that typically reflects expectations for more accommodative financial conditions. Historically, bond traders have anticipated changes in monetary policy well before equity markets adjust, especially during late-cycle slowdowns.¹ ²

Credit spreads, which measure the compensation investors demand for taking on default risk, remain well below levels typically associated with economic stress. This suggests lenders are not bracing for a sharp deterioration in corporate fundamentals. Futures pricing and central bank communications have reinforced this message, with markets increasingly expecting policy to remain steady or supportive rather than restrictive.³

When borrowing costs fall and credit availability improves, the effects tend to ripple through the economy unevenly. Rate-sensitive segments often respond first, while equity markets usually take longer to reflect improving financial conditions.

Why Small Caps Are More Sensitive to Credit Conditions

Small-cap companies are structurally more exposed to shifts in credit conditions than large-cap firms. Smaller businesses rely more heavily on bank lending and short-term financing rather than retained earnings or long-dated bond issuance. Federal Reserve surveys consistently show that traditional banks remain the primary funding source for small firms in the United States.⁴

A meaningful share of small-cap corporate debt is floating rate or matures within shorter time frames. When interest rates rise, financing costs increase quickly. When rates ease, the relief is often immediate. Research from major asset managers has shown that higher rates have been a disproportionate headwind for small-cap balance sheets in recent years.⁵

Bond market behavior reinforces this dynamic. Periods of tightening credit spreads have historically coincided with stronger relative performance in small-cap equities, particularly when spreads decline from elevated levels. Market research suggests that equity returns tend to improve when credit risk is perceived to be falling rather than rising.⁶

Valuations, Sentiment, and the Risk-Reward Trade-Off

Valuations add another layer to the case. Small-cap equities are trading at some of the widest discounts to large-cap stocks seen in decades on several long-term valuation measures.² This gap reflects years of underperformance, elevated financing costs, and investor preference for large, cash-rich companies with more stable margins.

Sentiment, however, can shift quickly when financial conditions change. Credit markets currently reflect low default expectations and generally stable corporate balance sheets, including within lower-rated segments of the market.

Data from high-yield credit markets show default rates remaining contained relative to historical averages, supporting a more constructive outlook for economically sensitive firms.⁷

Risks remain. Small-cap stocks tend to be more volatile, and any reversal in credit trends could quickly pressure earnings and valuations. Still, history suggests that when borrowing costs decline and credit spreads compress, smaller companies often respond earlier and more forcefully than the broader equity market. For investors paying close attention to credit conditions, small caps may be positioned to benefit first if the bond market’s signal proves correct.

Footnotes

  1. Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, “Corporate Bond Credit Spreads,” FRED Economic Data, accessed January 6, 2026.

  2. Franklin Templeton, “U.S. Small Caps Are (Finally!) on Top,” Royce Investment Partners, published October 9, 2025.

  3. CME Group, “Federal Funds Futures: Monetary Policy Expectations,” published December 13, 2024.

  4. Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, “2024 Small Business Credit Survey: Report on Employer Firms,” published August 14, 2024.

  5. State Street Global Advisors, “Why Small Caps Are More Sensitive to Interest Rates,” published May 22, 2024.

  6. Penn Capital Management, “Credit Spreads as a Leading Indicator for Equity Returns,” published March 18, 2024.

  7. Moody’s Investors Service, “U.S. High-Yield Default Forecast: Stable Credit Conditions Persist,” published November 7, 2024.

data.title: Melrose Industries: Strong Buy As Aerospace Growth Supports The Turnaround
data.description:
Melrose Industries PLC (MLSPF) has returned 10.9%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 2.5% gain since my last report, bringing the total gain since initiated coverage with a buy rating. In my view, the company has an attractive combination of near-term growth drivers as well as long-term revenue opportunities. The company provided a trading update in November, and in this report, I analyze the trading update and update my price target.

The Investment Thesis For Melrose Industries

Melrose Industries initially was a buy-improve-sell company that focused on acquiring businesses, optimizing those businesses, and then selling them. In 2018, the company acquired GKN, demerged the automotive and powder segments, resulting in an pure-play aerospace and defense company.

That company has two segments:

  1. Engines, which account for 45% of sales.
  2. Structures, which account for 55% of sales.

While the structures segment currently accounts for the biggest share of sales, it should be noted that the engines segment holds the highest growth rate. So, we see an appreciable mix between Engines and Structures. Besides the growth rate for Engines, we note that the segment derives around 56% of its sales from aftermarket services. Aftermarket services offer long-revenue tails with strong margins. As the number of aero engines in operations grows, so does the long-term value generation potential.

The company’s engine portfolio consists of legacy engine platforms such as the IAE V2500 used on the A320, the CFM56 used on the Boeing 737 and A320, and the GE90 used on the Boeing 777. The company also has exposure to the PW1000G used on the A320neo, A220, Embraer E2 and Comac C919 as well as the Trent XWB used on the A350 and the GEnx aero engine powering the Boeing 787 and Boeing 747-8. So, within the engine business, we not only see a nice mix between sales to manufacturers and aftermarket sales, but we also see an engine portfolio balanced between legacy and new platforms as well as single-aisle and wide-body airplanes.

In the structures segment, the company diversifies its end market with 66% of the sales for civil platforms and 34% for defense platforms.

That makes Melrose Industries a company that has near-term opportunities through its structures and engine sales with long-term revenue tails through its engine aftermarket business and, to a lesser extent, the structures business with commercial and defense end markets. In the years ahead, airplane production rates are set to increase, driving up OEM sales, while aftermarket sales currently are already strong. We note that long-term revenues from legacy platforms do taper over time, but over the life cycle of an engine program, the prices for parts are being escalated at a rate higher than inflation, making legacy programs highly profitable. That means that the company benefits from new engine platform rates being escalated, but the same happens for legacy platforms to the point that only in the end-of-life phase of the engine program life margin compression will occur. So, overall the business has a strong margin expansion and margin protection mechanism, providing an attractive long-term growth and investment opportunity. Furthermore, expanding defense budgets are driving demand for defense platforms.

The Risks For Melrose Industries

The opportunities for Melrose Industries are clear with a diverse portfolio and commercial and defense end-market growth. The risks, however, should also be highlighted:

  • Supply chain constraints, which limit airplane production rates, lead to lower than anticipated deliveries and slower growth in aftermarket sales.
  • Delays in increases for airplane productions.
  • Aftermarket sales growth slowing; Part of the growth is driven by airplanes and engines being held in service longer and requiring more maintenance and parts due to the shortage of airplanes, which will eventually diminish.
  • Trade and tariffs; The company derives around 39% of its revenue from the US and 61% in Europe.
  • Warranty and quality risk; Like any engineering company, there is the risk of claim exposure.

Below, I have provided a risk score for the aforementioned risk as well as some risks that are not necessarily unique to the business or aerospace businesses. The score is simply calculated using a probability score between 1 and 5 and an impact score between 1-5. A high probability or impact is scored higher. We see that the only elevated and high-risk currents are on supply chain constraints and airplane production rates. Other scores are generally medium or even low.

From here, we can also discuss some mitigations. For the supply chain constraints, mitigation measures include increasing investments throughout the supply chain and building enough inventory. OEM build-rate volatility is not something that can actively be managed by the company, but impacts can be managed through negotiations with manufacturers, keeping a lean model that can flex with demand.

Labor availability and cost risk can be mitigated through the implementation of automation, offering good pay and training to workers, while costs such as raw material costs can be managed through long-term contracts with fixed pricing.

Tariffs can be managed by adopting a local-for-local production footprint, leveraging trade agreements, exemptions, and renegotiation of commercial agreements, while forex risk can be hedged.

Trading Update Shows Strong Performance For Melrose Industries

In the November trading update, there were no major surprises. Group revenues from July through October grew 14%, with engine sales growing 28% inclusive of 22% growth in aftermarket sales and structure segment sales growing 5%. Backlog is supporting higher OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) output while there is a step change in defense spendings.

For the full year, the guidance has been maintained:

  • Revenue range of £3.425 billion to £3.575 billion
  • Free cash flow generation of £100+ million

Melrose Industries' Has Strong Financial Growth Ahead

For Melrose Industries, I am expecting revenue to be in the mid-single digits through 2027, driven by growth in commercial OEM, commercial aftermarket and defense sales. That will also drive operating leverage and bring in the high-margin proportion of aftermarket sales growth, driving up margins.

Cash flows have inflected in 2024 and in the years ahead, the cash flow conversion is expected to increase substantially to around 28%. I believe that these are still conversion rates that are well beyond what the company could achieve. So, beyond 2027 I would expect to see further upside to the free cash flow generation. The company is expected to return value to shareholders through shareholders, but for 2027 I am currently not assuming any share repurchases.

From net-debt leverage perspective, we see that the company is expected to remain within the 2x-2.5x range for 2025 and drop below that in 2026. So, there definitely is space for additional share repurchase beyond what I have modelled without substantially adding to the leverage. Beyond the current forecast period, I do expect that the company’s targets of 12% free-cash-flow-to-sales indicate a path towards over $750 million in free cash flow and 29% margins. So, the company is expected to see substantial improvements in its operational performance, but it is highly likely that in the years ahead there is additional upside to margins and free-cash-flow conversion.

Melrose Industries Has Substantial Upside As Recovery And Growth Ramp Up

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The stock price target for EBITDA has increased from $10.27 to $10.51 in the base case. We note that the company seemingly trades based on realized results rather than forward earnings, and that is why the current price target, which implies 19% upside, is based on expected earnings for 2025. However, with a forward looking assessment we would end up with 42% upside and a $12.56 price target, noting that the company trades substantially below peers. The trading range of 12x-12.5x EV/EBITDA is well below the company’s median of 14.4x and also below the peer group. We note that EBITDA estimates in dollar terms have improved by 1% for 2025, 1.7% for 2026, and 1.8% for 2027 for a total change of 1.5% and a CAGR of 15.1%. It should be noted that this is fully driven by translation from GBP to USD, and the actual EBITDA estimates have not materially changed. For free cash flow, we note that estimates for 2025 are slightly lower in dollars with a 7.8% downward revision for 2026 and 5.5% for 2027 driven by higher capital expenditures for a total downward revision of 4.6%. The price targets were partially lifted by the company median EV/EBITDA expanding from 14.3x to 14.4x.

Conclusion: Melrose Industries Has A lot Of Growth Ahead

I believe that Melrose Industries has substantial upside as the company grows sales in all its end markets, providing the company with increased operating leverage to drive margins up. Furthermore, as long as the higher-margin aftermarket sales growth outpaces lower margin commercial OEM sales, we also see a better margin mix as a driver of margin growth. I like the diversity of the business as the company is diversified along the end market axis as well as the portfolio axis and revenue timing axis. Furthermore, we note that the upside that we currently see is unlikely to showcase performance near the usual run rate and conversion ratios as the business is still very much in the recovery phase with free cash flow having turned positive and cash conversion now in the process of growing, providing substantial growth in results beyond 2027. As a result, I remain extremely bullish on the stock with a Strong Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
